title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: Thank interest contributing imuGAP! document explains report issues, propose changes, expect CI pipeline open pull request.

title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: contributors expected respectful professional interactions — issues, pull requests, code reviews, discussions. Constructive feedback welcome; personal attacks dismissive language .

title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: Use bug report template opening new issue. asks description, minimal reprex, sessionInfo() output, operating system.

title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: Use feature request template. Describe use case , possible, feature fits existing model workflow (imuGAP() -> Stan sampling -> post-processing). can also open blank issue neither template fits.

title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: Fork & branch. Create feature branch main (e.g. fix/obs-population-check feature/predict-mode). Make changes. Follow style conventions . Test locally. Run R CMD check test suite pushing (see commands ). Open pull request main clear description changed .

title: Contributing to imuGAP
code: # Full rebuild + install (required after editing any .stan file) R CMD INSTALL --preclean .  # Regenerate documentation from roxygen comments Rscript -e 'devtools::document()'  # Run the test suite Rscript -e 'devtools::test()'  # Lint the package (config in .lintr) Rscript -e 'lintr::lint_package()'  # Build and check R CMD build . && R CMD check imuGAP_*.tar.gz

title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: R code linted lintr (configuration .lintr). R/stanmodels.R inst/analysis/ excluded linting generated standalone scripts, respectively. hand-edit generated files: R/stanmodels.R, src/stanExports_*.{cc,h}, configure, configure.win produced rstantools::rstan_config(). Stan model variants composed via #include toggling top-level .stan files — prefer toggling includes duplicating whole model files.

what: What Happens When You Open a PR
title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: Every pull request triggers two GitHub Actions workflows: workflows pass PR merged.

title: Contributing to imuGAP
text: something unclear, open issue comment existing one — happy help.

what: imuGAP: an Estimation Model for Measles Vaccination Coverage
title: imuGAP: an Estimation Model for Measles Vaccination Coverage
text:
name imuGAP stands “Immunity: Geographic & Age-based Projection”, name implies, package provides stan-based model estimating measles vaccination coverage location age cohort. core model represents target population life-long propensity vaccination; proportion, ϕ\phi, population unlikely vaccinate complementary proportion, 1−ϕ1 - \phi, likely vaccinate. population experiences vaccination rate, λ\lambda, model time eras, according vaccination eligibility schedule, ν
u. core parameters can vary time location, user-specifiable way. Focusing just core model element, imagine particular population location ii cohort aa (aa denotes start time period group born). cohort now age tt, vaccine schedule first dose ν(t)
u(t), expected fraction group least one dose : P(≥1 dose)=(1−ϕi,)(1−exp{−∫atλi,(s)ν(s)ds}) P(\ge	extrm{1 dose}) = \left(1 - \phi_{, }ight) \left(1 - \exp\left\{-\int_a^{t} \lambda_{, }(s)
u(s) d	extrm{s}ight\}ight) say, representing vaccination coverage via survival model. Leaving aside “unlikely” population

title: Authors and Citation
text: Carl Pearson. Author, maintainer. Claire Perrin Smith. Author. Kelly Zhen. Contributor. Weston Voglesonger. Contributor. Joshua Chen. Contributor. Minjae Kung. Contributor.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Pearson C, Smith C (2026). imuGAP: Estimate Measles Vaccine Coverage. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://accidda.github.io/imuGAP/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {imuGAP: Estimate Measles Vaccine Coverage},   author = {Carl Pearson and Claire Perrin Smith},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://accidda.github.io/imuGAP/}, }

title: Estimate Measles Vaccine Coverage
text: imuGAP provides fitting imputation / prediction tool particular process model vaccination uptake. tool provides flexible trends relationships elements process.

title: Estimate Measles Vaccine Coverage
text: Requires R >= 4.1.0 C++ toolchain (Stan model compilation).

title: Estimate Measles Vaccine Coverage
text: imuGAP sampler fits two model effects: lifetime tendency towards vaccination time varying force vaccination. fit effects, model evaluates observations may combine many populations. model distinguishes populations place, birth timing, age. model organizes places hierarchy, example state contains counties turn contain cities. effects particular place combine impact enclosing locations.

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: functions validate, clean, convert raw user-supplied data structures (locations, observations, populations) canonical forms required [sampling()] sampler underlying Stan models.

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
code: canonicalize_locations(locations)  canonicalize_observations(observations, drop_extra = TRUE)  canonicalize_populations(   populations,   observations,   locations,   max_cohort,   max_age,   max_dose = 2L )

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: locations [data.frame()], columns loc_id parent_id, type. See Details restrictions. observations [data.frame()], observed data, least three columns: obs_id column; type, long unique, non-NA positive column; non-negative integers, observed number vaccinated individuals sample_n column; positive integers, number individuals sampled, must greater equal "positive" optionally, censored column; numeric, NA (uncensored) 1 (right-censored); present, assumed NA drop_extra logical scalar; drop extraneous columns? (default: yes) populations [data.frame()], observation meta data, columns obs_id, type; observation row concerns (.e. id shared observations data object) loc_id, type; location row concerns (.e. id shared locations data object) dose, non-zero, positive integer (1, 2, ...); dose row concerns cohort, positive integer; cohort location row concerns age, positive integer; age cohort row concerns weight, numeric, (0, 1); relative contribution row observation. Optional population row unique obs_id. max_cohort present, maximum cohort present? max_age present, maximum age present? max_dose maximum dose number allow (default: 2L)

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: canonicalize_locations returns data.table, : loc_id, parent_id columns originally supplied, possibly reordered loc_c_id, loc_cp_id columns, canonicalized id/parent_id columns, representing order used sampler layer column, integer 1 (root), 2 (root children), 3 (grandchildren), &c layer_bound column, integer starting 1 layer. provides index slice information used stan model. canonicalize_observations returns canonical observation object, [data.table()] : obs_c_id column, integer sequence 1; order observations passed estimation original obs_id column, possibly reordered positive sample_n columns, possibly reordered "censored" column; NA, present original observations argument canonicalize_populations returns canonical populations object, mirroring input populations, following updates: obs_c_id, observation id row concerns, canonicalized match canonical observation ids loc_c_id, location id row concerns, canonicalized match reordered obs_c_id order

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: imuGAP hierarchical modeling framework requires data structures adhere specific relational format constraints. three canonicalize functions process validate inputs described :

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: [sampling()] sampler works hierarchical model locations, must provided structure. method checks location structure validity, returns canonical version including layer membership. valid structure : unique root, cycles, duplicate loc_ids Users may explicitly identify root loc_id providing row parent_id equal NA. Otherwise, parent_id appear loc_id treated root. input valid, method create canonicalized version. version, ids run 1:N, N number distinct ids. order determined layer order, position parent within layer, "natural" order (.e., whatever base R sort() yields).

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: observations object documents observations used fit model. Conceptually, row represents observation vaccination status within population. population need uniform (see [canonicalize_populations()]) concerning single cohort time: observation generally best available resolution data. resolution can vary across rows. sampler uses information resolutions automatically figure compare latent process model different observations. optional censored column: model supports vaccination status indicators vaccine specific well represent individual set vaccines (including target vaccine). specific coverage target vaccine right-censored latter case: full-set-coverage minimum coverage target. least data censored, must supply censored column correctly estimate coverage. Mark uncensored observations NA, right-censored observations 1. Note 0 valid value time; preserving potential future support left-censoring.

title: Canonicalize imuGAP Data Objects — canonicalize
text: method validates meta-data associated observations, well converting meta-data use canonical id formats. Regarding "cohorts" "ages": counted 1, 1 "unit". can imagine units whatever resolution appropriate data: months, quarters, years, etc. long used consistently, estimation work, take unit meaning used input.

what: Create target population for prediction — create_target
title: Create target population for prediction — create_target
text: Creates target object appropriate use [predict.imugap_fit()], used specific imugap_fit object.

title: Create target population for prediction — create_target
code: create_target(   fit,   location,   age,   cohort,   dose,   mode = c("error", "enumerate", "recycle") )

title: Create target population for prediction — create_target
text: fit imugap_fit object returned [sampling()] location either vector locations data.frame; vector locations, treated target locations; data.frame, validated target object. age vector ages predict coverage, consistent [canonicalize_populations()] cohort vector cohorts predict coverage, consistent [canonicalize_populations()] dose vector doses predict coverage, consistent [canonicalize_observations()]

title: Create target population for prediction — create_target
text: data.table representing canonicalized target population.

title: Create target population for prediction — create_target
text: location data.frame, function validates object fit argument. Non-missing values arguments error approach. Otherwise, location must correspond vector location IDs age, cohort, dose must also supplied. Depending mode argument, arguments may different lengths. mode = "error" (default), arguments must length. mode = "enumerate", resulting target combinations arguments. mode = "recycle", resulting target recycle arguments least-common-multiple length.

title: Custom imuGAP fit extraction — extract_imugap
text: Thin wrapper around rstan::extract extract typical imuGAP parameters.

title: Custom imuGAP fit extraction — extract_imugap
text: fit imugap_fit object returned sampling() pars character vector; parameters extract. Defaults "beta_bs", state-level B-spline parameter. ... additional arguments passed [rstan::extract()].

title: Custom imuGAP fit extraction — extract_imugap
text: list, returned rstan::extract()

title: Example Stan Fit — fit_sim
text: reference stanfit object produced running imuGAP() bundled locations_sim, populations_sim, observations_sim datasets. Intended lightweight fixture examples, tests, downstream tooling needs real fit without paying cost recompiling re-running Stan model.

title: Example Stan Fit — fit_sim
text: stanfit object returned rstan::sampling().

title: Example Stan Fit — fit_sim
text: Generated minimal sampler settings smoke test: iter = 100 chains = 1 seed = 1L refresh = 0 settings enough convergence; fit_sim wiring fixture, scientifically meaningful posterior. generating script lives data-raw/fit_sim.R can re-run package root Rscript data-raw/fit_sim.R. Note stanfit objects bundle references compiled Stan model can sensitive major version changes rstan StanHeaders. future install fails load fit_sim, regenerate via data-raw/fit_sim.R.

title: imuGAP Model Options — imugap_options
text: function encapsulates option passing imuGAP settings.

title: imuGAP Model Options — imugap_options
text: df degrees freedom use bspline dose_schedule integer vector, ages dose(s) n scheduled, vector indices doses matching object stan model object use; currently "default" supported

title: imuGAP Model Options — imugap_options
text: list imuGAP model options

title: imuGAP Model Options — imugap_options
code: imugap_options() #> $df #> [1] 5 #>  #> $dose_schedule #> [1] 1 4 #>  #> $object #> S4 class stanmodel 'impute_school_coverage_process_v6' coded as follows: #> functions { #>   // a function to convert lower bounds l_1, 1_2, ... 1_n #>   // to (lower, upper) pairs (l_1, l_2-1), (l_2, l_3-1), ... #>   array[,] int bounds_to_range(array[] int lowers, int ub) { #>     int size_bounds = size(lowers); #>     if (lowers[size_bounds] > ub) { #>         print("Upper bound, ", ub, " is less than last lower bound, ", lowers[size_bounds]); #>     } #>     array[size_bounds] int uppers; #>     for (i in 1:(size_bounds - 1)) { #>         uppers[i] = lowers[i+1] - 1; #>     } #>     uppers[size_bounds] = ub; #>     return { lowers, uppers }; #>   } #>   // create a matrix, each column multiplied by corresponding row entry #>   matrix element_mult_expand(vector colv, row_vector rowv) { #>     int nrows = size(colv), ncols = size(rowv); #>     matrix[nrows, ncols] result; #>     for (i in 1:nrows) { #>         result[i,] = rowv * colv[i]; #>     } #>     return result; #>   } #>   // Sequential diff #>   vector diff(vector obj) { #>     int sz = size(obj); #>     return obj[2:] - obj[:(sz-1)]; #>   } #>   row_vector diff(row_vector obj) { #>     int sz = size(obj); #>     return obj[2:] - obj[:(sz-1)]; #>   } #>   row_vector colsum(matrix obj) { #>     int ncols = cols(obj); #>     row_vector[ncols] res; #>     for (i in 1:ncols) { #>         res[i] = sum(obj[, i]); #>     } #>     return res; #>   } #>   vector rowsum(matrix obj) { #>     int nrows = rows(obj); #>     vector[nrows] res; #>     for (i in 1:nrows) { #>         res[i] = sum(obj[i, ]); #>     } #>     return res; #>   } #> vector unrolled_dose(int n_yr, int n_doses, matrix dose_sched, vector lambda_raw, real epsilon_p) { #>   // assert: dose_sched is n_yr x n_doses #>   // assert: lambda_raw is n_doses x 1 (alt: would be n_doses x n_time) #>   vector[n_doses] lambda = exp(lambda_raw); #>   // the unconditional cdfs, given FoV + whatever remains; normalization factors #>   matrix[n_yr, n_doses] dXcdf, dXpdf, normdXpdf; #>   vector[n_doses] rem; #>   for (dose in 1:n_doses) { #>     dXcdf[, dose] = 1 - exp(-cumulative_sum(dose_sched[, dose] * lambda[dose])); #>     rem[dose] = 1 - dXcdf[n_yr, dose]; #>     dXpdf[1, dose] = dXcdf[1, dose]; #>     dXpdf[2:,dose] = diff(dXcdf[, dose]); #>     normdXpdf[, dose] = reverse(cumulative_sum(reverse(dXpdf[, dose]))) + rem[dose]; #>   } #>   matrix[n_yr, n_doses] conditional_dXpdf = rep_matrix(0, n_yr, n_doses), conditional_dXcdf; #>   conditional_dXpdf[, 1] = dXpdf[, 1]; #>   conditional_dXcdf[, 1] = dXcdf[, 1]; #>   for (dose in 2:n_doses) { #>     int prev_dose = dose - 1; #>     // conditional probability => probability got dose n-1 at some time, then probability got dose n at later times #>     for (ly in 1:n_yr) { #>       if (normdXpdf[ly, dose] < epsilon_p) break; // if the remaining weight is negligible, avoid division by zero #>       conditional_dXpdf[ly:, dose] += conditional_dXpdf[ly, prev_dose] * dXpdf[ly:, dose] / normdXpdf[ly, dose]; #>     } #>     conditional_dXcdf[, dose] = cumulative_sum(conditional_dXpdf[, dose]); #>   } #>   // TODO check unrolling of cdfs - should be dose 1 all years, then dose 2 all years, etc #>   vector[n_doses * n_yr] unrolled_dose_cdf = to_vector(conditional_dXcdf); #>   return unrolled_dose_cdf; #> } #> } #> // need meta info: #> //  which cohorts, which places, which years of life #> //  => assume each measurement is independent, not progress of some matched individuals #> data { #> // #include data/shared_stateonly.stan #>   // STRUCTURAL DEFINITIONS #>   int<lower=1> n_yr; // number of years to model for each cohort - should be at least year of oldest observation #>   int<lower=1> n_cohort; // number of birth year cohorts #>   int<lower=1> n_sch; // number of schools #>  #>   // maps schools to county #>   int<lower=1> n_cnty; #>   array[n_cnty] int<lower=1, upper=n_sch> cnty_bounds; // which schools indices start each county #>   // dose schedules #>   int<lower=1> n_doses; #>   matrix<lower=0, upper=1>[n_yr, n_doses] dose_sched; #>   // DATA DEFINITIONS #>   int<lower=1> n_obs; #>   int<lower=0> y_obs[n_obs]; #>   int<lower=0> y_smp[n_obs]; #>   // have school id ranges for observations & for doses; school id 0 == statewide? #>   // int obs_sch_id_bounds[n_obs]; #>   int<lower=n_obs> n_weights; #>   array[n_obs] int<lower=1, upper=n_weights> obs_to_weights_bounds; // each entry is the start of the range #>   int<lower=1,upper=n_sch + n_cnty + 1> weights_school[n_weights]; #>   int<lower=1,upper=n_cohort> weights_cohort[n_weights]; #>   int<lower=1,upper=n_yr> weights_life_year[n_weights]; #>   int<lower=1,upper=n_doses> weights_dose[n_weights]; #>   vector<lower=0,upper=1>[n_weights] weights; // contribution of this (school, cohort, year, dose) to an observation #>   // run mode: 0 = estimation, 1 = prediction #>   int<lower=0, upper=1> predict_mode; #>   // TODO: calculate these in stan? #>   // https://spinkney.github.io/helpful_stan_functions/group__splines.html #>   // state-level basis spline #>   int k_bs; // number of bspline basis functions #>   matrix[n_cohort, k_bs] bs; // basis functions #> # observations may be right-censored #> # observation data is assumed ordered uncensored, then right censored #> # so n_uncensored_obs == n_obs, all observations are uncensored #> # number of uncensored observations #> int<lower = 0, upper = n_obs> n_uncensored_obs; #> } #> transformed data { #>    // #include transformed_data/stateonly.stan #>    // #include transformed_data/fixed_raw_lambda.stan #>    // #include transformed_data/fixed_logit_phi.stan #> real epsilon_p = 1e-10; #>   // convert to lookup spans for convenience #>   array[2, n_cnty] int cnty_map = bounds_to_range(cnty_bounds, n_sch); #>   array[2, n_obs] int obs_map = bounds_to_range(obs_to_weights_bounds, n_weights); #>  #>   // Equivalent 1:n_cohort, for time trends #>   vector[n_cohort] cohort_shift_counter = linspaced_vector(n_cohort, 1, n_cohort); #>   int<lower=1> phi_lookup[n_weights]; #>   int<lower=1> cdf_lookup[n_weights]; #>   // because integer arrays don't support broadcasting ... #>   // unroll phi and cdf objects to support vectorization #>   for (weight_i in 1:n_weights) { #>     // phi ordered by school then cohort #>     phi_lookup[weight_i] = weights_cohort[weight_i] + (weights_school[weight_i] - 1) * n_cohort; #>     // ordered by dose then life year #>     cdf_lookup[weight_i] = weights_life_year[weight_i] + (weights_dose[weight_i] - 1) * n_yr; #>   } #> int<lower=-1> y_obs_trans[n_obs]; #> for(i in 1:n_obs) { #>   y_obs_trans[i] = y_obs[i] - 1; #> } #> } #> parameters { #>   // bases spline coeficcients #>   vector[k_bs] beta_bs; // spline betas #>   // #include parameters/constant_phi.stan #>   // #include parameters/cnty_sch_linear.stan #>   // offsets #>   real<lower=0> sigma_cnty; #>   row_vector<multiplier=sigma_cnty>[n_cnty] off_cnty; #>   real<lower=0> sigma_sch; #>   row_vector<multiplier=sigma_sch>[n_sch] off_sch; #>   // Vaccination uptake rate #>   vector[n_doses] lambda_raw; #> } #> model { #>   if (!predict_mode) { #>     // PRIORS - spline coefficients #>     beta_bs ~ normal(0, 10); #>     // PRIOR - lambda; relatively strong prior belief that ~95% coverage achieved in a year #>     // mean of 3 => 1 - exp(-3*1) == ~ 0.95 #>     lambda_raw ~ normal(log(3), 1); #>     // Offsets - non-centered parameterization to speed up #>     sigma_cnty ~ cauchy(0, 1); #>     off_cnty ~ normal(0, sigma_cnty); #>     sigma_sch ~ cauchy(0, 2); #>     off_sch ~ normal(0, sigma_sch); #>     vector[n_obs] p_obs; #> vector[n_cohort] logit_phi_st = bs * beta_bs; #> row_vector[n_sch] shift = off_sch; #> for (c in 1:n_cnty) { #>   shift[cnty_map[1,c]:cnty_map[2,c]] += off_cnty[c]; #> } #> vector[(1+ n_cnty + n_sch) * n_cohort] phi = append_row(append_row( #>   inv_logit(logit_phi_st), #>   to_vector(inv_logit( #>     rep_matrix(logit_phi_st, n_cnty) + rep_matrix(off_cnty, n_cohort) #>   ))), #>   to_vector(inv_logit( #>     rep_matrix(logit_phi_st, n_sch) + rep_matrix(shift, n_cohort) #>   )) #> ); #> vector[n_doses * n_yr] unrolled_dose_probs = unrolled_dose(n_yr, n_doses, dose_sched, lambda_raw, epsilon_p); #> vector[n_weights] weighted = (1 - phi[phi_lookup]) .* unrolled_dose_probs[cdf_lookup] .* weights; #> for (obs_i in 1:n_obs) { #>   p_obs[obs_i] = sum(weighted[obs_map[1,obs_i]:obs_map[2,obs_i]]); #> } #> if (n_uncensored_obs < n_obs) { # at least some censored observations #>     # p_s => 1 - p_s = p_f :: probability of at least this many successes => #>     #                         probability of less than this many failures #>     if (n_uncensored_obs > 0) { # at least some uncensored observations #>         target += binomial_lpmf(y_obs[:n_uncensored_obs] | y_smp[:n_uncensored_obs], p_obs[:n_uncensored_obs]); #>     } #>     target += binomial_lcdf(y_obs[(n_uncensored_obs+1):] | y_smp[(n_uncensored_obs+1):], 1 - p_obs[(n_uncensored_obs+1):]); #> } else { # all uncensored observations #>     y_obs ~ binomial(y_smp, p_obs); # vectorized #> } #>   } #> } #> generated quantities { #>   vector[predict_mode ? n_obs : 0] p_obs; #>   if (predict_mode) { #> vector[n_cohort] logit_phi_st = bs * beta_bs; #> row_vector[n_sch] shift = off_sch; #> for (c in 1:n_cnty) { #>   shift[cnty_map[1,c]:cnty_map[2,c]] += off_cnty[c]; #> } #> vector[(1+ n_cnty + n_sch) * n_cohort] phi = append_row(append_row( #>   inv_logit(logit_phi_st), #>   to_vector(inv_logit( #>     rep_matrix(logit_phi_st, n_cnty) + rep_matrix(off_cnty, n_cohort) #>   ))), #>   to_vector(inv_logit( #>     rep_matrix(logit_phi_st, n_sch) + rep_matrix(shift, n_cohort) #>   )) #> ); #> vector[n_doses * n_yr] unrolled_dose_probs = unrolled_dose(n_yr, n_doses, dose_sched, lambda_raw, epsilon_p); #> vector[n_weights] weighted = (1 - phi[phi_lookup]) .* unrolled_dose_probs[cdf_lookup] .* weights; #> for (obs_i in 1:n_obs) { #>   p_obs[obs_i] = sum(weighted[obs_map[1,obs_i]:obs_map[2,obs_i]]); #> } #>   } #> } #> // This model represents vaccination as a discrete step, fixed hazard process #> // therefore X(t) => X(t + deltaT) = X(t)*exp(-hazard*deltaT) #> // which means the X-out flow == X(t)*(1-exp(-hazard*deltaT)) #> // #> // for an arbitrary number of hazards & deltaTs, we simply sum: #> // net outflow: X(t)*(1-exp(-sum(hazard*deltaT))) #> // #> // This model represents an overall hazard, shared across birth cohorts and #> // vaccine doses. Additionally, birth cohorts have a propensity to vaccinate, #> // which is fixed at birth and then applies throughout life. #> // #> // deltaT captures both time passage + eligibility. deltaT in the fitting is #> // always == 1, but note that this is unitless - the data define what time means #> // e.g. if birth cohorts are based on years, then deltaT is years, and a weight #> // of 1 means eligible for a whole year, .25 eligible for a quarter, etc #> // #> // the hazard is constant for any given discrete time step, but can vary between #> // time steps. the hazard time is denoted in absolute model time. however, #> // cohort time is relative. so: #> // hazard time == 1: one deltaT has passed, absolute time is 1 #> // cohort 1, time 1: one deltaT has passed => cohort 1 is 1 deltaT old, #> //   absolute time is also 1 (cohort 1 is the first cohort) #> // cohort 2, time 2: two deltaT have passed => cohort 2 is 2 deltaT old, but #> //   cohort 1 is 3 deltaT old, and absolute time is 3 #> // generally: #> // cohort n, time t: experienced t deltaTs; those deltaT corresponded to absolute #> //   times n, n+1, ..., n+t #> // #> // we assume each cohort has the same doses schedule, so the same deltaT weights #> // #> // so for cohort n, fraction of (non-phi/vaccinators) with 1+ doses at age 1:t: #> //   d1cdf = 1 - exp(-cumsum(weight[1:t] .* lambda[n:t])) #> // #> // and the incidence of new dose 1s is: #> // #> //   c(d1cdf[1], diff(d1cdf)) #> // #> // for subsequent doses, the hazard model is the same, but now is conditional on #> // having received the first dose; thus the incremental hazard is still #> //   d2cdf = 1 - exp(-cumsum(weight2[1:t] .* lambda[n:t])) #> //   (note the different eligibility mask, weight2; assert weight2 strictly less than weight1) #> // but only the fraction having dose 1 experience it. #> // if we think about this in terms of newly-dose-1-in-deltaT-x: #> //   we can reframe in these terms: #> //   define d2pdf = c(d2cdf[1], diff(d2cdf)); pgtt = 1-d2cdf[t] (probably event has not occured by t) #> //   probability of dose 2 in year (x+1):t => d2pdf[(x+1):t]/sum(d2pdf[x+1:t] + pggt) #> //   we can then assign each incremental incidence of dose 1 in deltaT x to getting #> //   second dose in deltaT x+1:t (or not getting it by t) #> // #> // this can be built-up as a matrix once per cohort #> //  1. calculate d2cdf, d2pdf, pgtt as above, for t == max cohort "age" #> //  2. calculate remd2pdf = rev(cumsum(rev(d2pdf))) + pgtt #> //  3. d2contrib = #> //    upper_triangle_1s * colwise element multiplication of d2pdf / rowwise remd2pdf * rowwise d2incidence => column sums => proportion in d2  #>  imugap_options(dose_schedule = c(1, 3)) #> $df #> [1] 5 #>  #> $dose_schedule #> [1] 1 3 #>  #> $object #> S4 class stanmodel 'impute_school_coverage_process_v6' coded as follows: #> functions { #>   // a function to convert lower bounds l_1, 1_2, ... 1_n #>   // to (lower, upper) pairs (l_1, l_2-1), (l_2, l_3-1), ... #>   array[,] int bounds_to_range(array[] int lowers, int ub) { #>     int size_bounds = size(lowers); #>     if (lowers[size_bounds] > ub) { #>         print("Upper bound, ", ub, " is less than last lower bound, ", lowers[size_bounds]); #>     } #>     array[size_bounds] int uppers; #>     for (i in 1:(size_bounds - 1)) { #>         uppers[i] = lowers[i+1] - 1; #>     } #>     uppers[size_bounds] = ub; #>     return { lowers, uppers }; #>   } #>   // create a matrix, each column multiplied by corresponding row entry #>   matrix element_mult_expand(vector colv, row_vector rowv) { #>     int nrows = size(colv), ncols = size(rowv); #>     matrix[nrows, ncols] result; #>     for (i in 1:nrows) { #>         result[i,] = rowv * colv[i]; #>     } #>     return result; #>   } #>   // Sequential diff #>   vector diff(vector obj) { #>     int sz = size(obj); #>     return obj[2:] - obj[:(sz-1)]; #>   } #>   row_vector diff(row_vector obj) { #>     int sz = size(obj); #>     return obj[2:] - obj[:(sz-1)]; #>   } #>   row_vector colsum(matrix obj) { #>     int ncols = cols(obj); #>     row_vector[ncols] res; #>     for (i in 1:ncols) { #>         res[i] = sum(obj[, i]); #>     } #>     return res; #>   } #>   vector rowsum(matrix obj) { #>     int nrows = rows(obj); #>     vector[nrows] res; #>     for (i in 1:nrows) { #>         res[i] = sum(obj[i, ]); #>     } #>     return res; #>   } #> vector unrolled_dose(int n_yr, int n_doses, matrix dose_sched, vector lambda_raw, real epsilon_p) { #>   // assert: dose_sched is n_yr x n_doses #>   // assert: lambda_raw is n_doses x 1 (alt: would be n_doses x n_time) #>   vector[n_doses] lambda = exp(lambda_raw); #>   // the unconditional cdfs, given FoV + whatever remains; normalization factors #>   matrix[n_yr, n_doses] dXcdf, dXpdf, normdXpdf; #>   vector[n_doses] rem; #>   for (dose in 1:n_doses) { #>     dXcdf[, dose] = 1 - exp(-cumulative_sum(dose_sched[, dose] * lambda[dose])); #>     rem[dose] = 1 - dXcdf[n_yr, dose]; #>     dXpdf[1, dose] = dXcdf[1, dose]; #>     dXpdf[2:,dose] = diff(dXcdf[, dose]); #>     normdXpdf[, dose] = reverse(cumulative_sum(reverse(dXpdf[, dose]))) + rem[dose]; #>   } #>   matrix[n_yr, n_doses] conditional_dXpdf = rep_matrix(0, n_yr, n_doses), conditional_dXcdf; #>   conditional_dXpdf[, 1] = dXpdf[, 1]; #>   conditional_dXcdf[, 1] = dXcdf[, 1]; #>   for (dose in 2:n_doses) { #>     int prev_dose = dose - 1; #>     // conditional probability => probability got dose n-1 at some time, then probability got dose n at later times #>     for (ly in 1:n_yr) { #>       if (normdXpdf[ly, dose] < epsilon_p) break; // if the remaining weight is negligible, avoid division by zero #>       conditional_dXpdf[ly:, dose] += conditional_dXpdf[ly, prev_dose] * dXpdf[ly:, dose] / normdXpdf[ly, dose]; #>     } #>     conditional_dXcdf[, dose] = cumulative_sum(conditional_dXpdf[, dose]); #>   } #>   // TODO check unrolling of cdfs - should be dose 1 all years, then dose 2 all years, etc #>   vector[n_doses * n_yr] unrolled_dose_cdf = to_vector(conditional_dXcdf); #>   return unrolled_dose_cdf; #> } #> } #> // need meta info: #> //  which cohorts, which places, which years of life #> //  => assume each measurement is independent, not progress of some matched individuals #> data { #> // #include data/shared_stateonly.stan #>   // STRUCTURAL DEFINITIONS #>   int<lower=1> n_yr; #> vector[n_doses * n_yr] unrolled_dose_probs = unrolled_dose(n_yr, n_doses, dose_sched, lambda_raw, epsilon_p); #> vector[n_weights] weighted = (1 - phi[phi_lookup]) .* unrolled_dose_probs[cdf_lookup] .* weights; #> for (obs_i in 1:n_obs) { #>   p_obs[obs_i] = sum(weighted[obs_map[1,obs_i]:obs_map[2,obs_i]]); #> } #>   } #> } #> // This model represents vaccination as a discrete step, fixed hazard process #> // therefore X(t) => X(t + deltaT) = X(t)*exp(-hazard*deltaT) #> // which means the X-out flow == X(t)*(1-exp(-hazard*deltaT)) #> // #> // for an arbitrary number of hazards & deltaTs, we simply sum: #> // net outflow: X(t)*(1-exp(-sum(hazard*deltaT))) #> // #> // This model represents an overall hazard, shared across birth cohorts and #> // vaccine doses. Additionally, birth cohorts have a propensity to vaccinate, #> // which is fixed at birth and then applies throughout life. #> // #> // deltaT captures both time passage + eligibility. deltaT in the fitting is #> // always == 1, but note that this is unitless - the data define what time means #> // e.g. if birth cohorts are based on years, then deltaT is years, and a weight #> // of 1 means eligible for a whole year, .25 eligible for a quarter, etc #> // #> // the hazard is constant for any given discrete time step, but can vary between #> // time steps. the hazard time is denoted in absolute model time. however, #> // cohort time is relative. so: #> // hazard time == 1: one deltaT has passed, absolute time is 1 #> // cohort 1, time 1: one deltaT has passed => cohort 1 is 1 deltaT old, #> //   absolute time is also 1 (cohort 1 is the first cohort) #> // cohort 2, time 2: two deltaT have passed => cohort 2 is 2 deltaT old, but #> //   cohort 1 is 3 deltaT old, and absolute time is 3 #> // generally: #> // cohort n, time t: experienced t deltaTs; those deltaT corresponded to absolute #> //   times n, n+1, ..., n+t #> // #> // we assume each cohort has the same doses schedule, so the same deltaT weights #> // #> // so for cohort n, fraction of (non-phi/vaccinators) with 1+ doses at age 1:t: #> //   d1cdf = 1 - exp(-cumsum(weight[1:t] .* lambda[n:t])) #> // #> // and the incidence of new dose 1s is: #> // #> //   c(d1cdf[1], diff(d1cdf)) #> // #> // for subsequent doses, the hazard model is the same, but now is conditional on #> // having received the first dose; thus the incremental hazard is still #> //   d2cdf = 1 - exp(-cumsum(weight2[1:t] .* lambda[n:t])) #> //   (note the different eligibility mask, weight2; assert weight2 strictly less than weight1) #> // but only the fraction having dose 1 experience it. #> // if we think about this in terms of newly-dose-1-in-deltaT-x: #> //   we can reframe in these terms: #> //   define d2pdf = c(d2cdf[1], diff(d2cdf)); pgtt = 1-d2cdf[t] (probably event has not occured by t) #> //   probability of dose 2 in year (x+1):t => d2pdf[(x+1):t]/sum(d2pdf[x+1:t] + pggt) #> //   we can then assign each incremental incidence of dose 1 in deltaT x to getting #> //   second dose in deltaT x+1:t (or not getting it by t) #> // #> // this can be built-up as a matrix once per cohort #> //  1. calculate d2cdf, d2pdf, pgtt as above, for t == max cohort "age" #> //  2. calculate remd2pdf = rev(cumsum(rev(d2pdf))) + pgtt #> //  3. d2contrib = #> //    upper_triangle_1s * colwise element multiplication of d2pdf / rowwise remd2pdf * rowwise d2incidence => column sums => proportion in d2  #>

title: Immunity: Geographic & Age-based Projection, imuGAP — sampling
text: observations [data.frame()], observed data, least three columns: obs_id column; type, long unique, non-NA positive column; non-negative integers, observed number vaccinated individuals sample_n column; positive integers, number individuals sampled, must greater equal "positive" optionally, censored column; numeric, NA (uncensored) 1 (right-censored); present, assumed NA populations [data.frame()], observation meta data, columns obs_id, type; observation row concerns (.e. id shared observations data object) loc_id, type; location row concerns (.e. id shared locations data object) dose, non-zero, positive integer (1, 2, ...); dose row concerns cohort, positive integer; cohort location row concerns age, positive integer; age cohort row concerns weight, numeric, (0, 1); relative contribution row observation. Optional population row unique obs_id. locations [data.frame()], columns loc_id parent_id, type. See Details restrictions. imugap_opts options imuGAP model stan_opts passed rstan::sampling (e.g. iter, chains).

title: Stan Sampler Options — stan_options
text: function encapsulates option passing stan sampler, exception model object, passed imugap_options.

title: Stan Sampler Options — stan_options
text: ... Arguments passed rstan::sampling data named list environment     providing data model character vector     names objects used data.     See Passing data Stan section stan. pars vector character strings specifying parameters interest.     default NA indicating parameters model.     include = TRUE, samples parameters named pars     stored fitted results. Conversely, include = FALSE,     samples parameters except named pars     stored fitted results. chains positive integer specifying number Markov chains.     default 4. iter positive integer specifying number iterations     chain (including warmup). default 2000. warmup positive integer specifying number warmup (aka burnin)     iterations per chain. step-size adaptation (default),     also controls number iterations adaptation run (    hence warmup samples used inference). number     warmup iterations smaller iter default     iter/2. thin positive integer specifying period saving samples.     default 1, usually recommended value. seed seed random number generation. default generated     1 maximum integer supported R machine. Even     multiple chains used, one seed needed, chains     seeds derived first chain avoid dependent samples.     seed specified number, .integer applied .     .integer produces NA, seed generated randomly.     seed can also specified character string digits,     "12345", converted integer. init Initial values specification. See detailed documentation     init argument stan. check_data Logical, defaulting TRUE. TRUE     data preprocessed; otherwise .     See Passing data Stan section stan. sample_file optional character string providing name file.     specified draws parameters saved quantities     written file. provided, files created.     folder specified writable, tempdir() used.     multiple chains, underscore chain number appended     file name prior .csv extension. diagnostic_file optional character string providing name file.     specified diagnostics data parameters written     file. provided, files created. folder specified     writable, tempdir() used. multiple chains,     underscore chain number appended file name prior     .csv extension. verbose TRUE FALSE: flag indicating whether     print intermediate output Stan console, might     helpful model debugging. algorithm One sampling algorithms implemented Stan.     Current options "NUTS" (-U-Turn sampler, Hoffman Gelman 2011, Betancourt 2017),     "HMC" (static HMC), "Fixed_param". default     preferred algorithm "NUTS". control named list parameters control sampler's     behavior. See details documentation control argument     stan. include Logical scalar defaulting TRUE indicating     whether include exclude parameters given     pars argument. FALSE, entire multidimensional     parameters can excluded, rather particular elements . cores Number cores use executing chains parallel,     defaults 1 recommend setting mc.cores option     many processors hardware RAM allow (    number chains). open_progress Logical scalar takes effect     cores > 1 recommended TRUE interactive     use progress chains redirected file     automatically opened inspection. short runs,     user might prefer FALSE. show_messages Either logical scalar (defaulting TRUE)     indicating whether print summary Informational Messages     screen chain finished character string naming path     summary stored. Setting FALSE recommended     unless sure model correct numerical     error.
